glossaries.label: Design Terms
glossaries.intro: Vocabulary to name what you see — and to pinpoint exactly what you want in your own designs. Includes the techniques and styles behind the sites in the journal.

terms.def: A page that tells a story as you scroll, with text and visuals changing in sync — great for narrative product walkthroughs.
A section stays fixed in place while content or animation plays over it, then the page continues. (Often the ‘scrolling effect’ people can't name.)
An animation whose progress is tied to scroll position, so scrolling forward/back plays it forward/back.
Elements fade, slide, or scale in as they enter the viewport.
Background and foreground layers move at different speeds on scroll, creating depth.
Overriding normal scrolling to control the pace or snapping of sections — powerful but can hurt usability, so use carefully.
The page snaps cleanly to each section as you scroll.
Content that moves sideways, often while the page scrolls down.
A short branded animation on first load before content appears (e.g. Steven.com's logo draw-on with a counter).
An animated change between pages or views instead of an instant cut.
A list or grid whose items animate in one after another rather than all at once.
Text that moves or animates as a design element.
Text or images revealed through a moving mask or wipe.
A continuously scrolling strip of text or logos.
A button that subtly pulls toward the cursor on hover.
A replaced or trailing cursor used as a brand or interaction flourish.
Content (an image, a detail) that appears only on hover.
A line or logo that appears to draw itself stroke by stroke.
A slowly shifting multi-color gradient used as a signature backdrop (e.g. Stripe's hero).
Stripped-back: lots of whitespace, few elements, restrained color.
Magazine-like — strong typography, columns, generous space, often serif/display type.
Raw, high-contrast, system fonts, exposed structure, intentionally ‘undesigned.’
Bold, dense, layered — lots of color and decoration.
Grid-based, clean sans-serif, objective and highly ordered.
Frosted, semi-transparent ‘glass’ panels with background blur.
Soft, extruded shapes that look pressed into or out of the background.
A grid of varied-size boxes, like a bento tray, each holding one feature.
Deliberately off-grid, overlapping, or asymmetric layouts for energy.
Imagery or color that runs edge to edge with no margins.
The viewport divided into two strong halves.
One saturated color floods the whole surface.
A dark background with light text and content.
Moody, photographic, film-like — strong imagery, deep contrast, sparse type (e.g. Aperture).
An oversized hand-drawn or brush-script word as the hero focal point — expressive and organic; adds personality and energy (good for lifestyle, sport, or craft brands).
A massive, heavy display word dominating the hero, often bigger than the imagery — an instant, confident statement of the brand's voice.
A magazine-style hero: multi-column text, refined serif or grotesk, small eyebrow labels, and lots of restraint — reads intellectual and premium.
A large headline sits behind the subject or product, which overlaps and pops in front — creates depth and literally ties the word to the product.
A headline set in outline only (stroke, no fill) for a lighter, graphic, architectural feel.
A word broken or interrupted by an image, a line, or the edge of the frame, implying the brand is bigger than the canvas.
An invisible framework of columns and rows used to align elements consistently.
Columns are the vertical divisions of a grid; the gutter is the gap between them.
The empty space around and between elements; it gives a design room to breathe and guides focus.
The order in which the eye notices things, created with size, weight, color, and spacing.
Lining elements up to a shared edge or axis so a layout feels intentional.
The part of a page visible before scrolling — prime real estate for the key message.
A wrapper that caps how wide content gets so lines stay readable on large screens.
Whether an element spans the whole viewport or sits inside the centered content column.
A typeface is the design (e.g. Helvetica); a font is a specific style/size of it.
Serifs are the small feet on letters; sans-serif faces have none and read as more modern.
How thick the strokes are — Light, Regular, Bold, etc.
The vertical space between lines of text.
The uniform spacing applied between all letters in a block of text.
Adjusting the space between specific letter pairs for even spacing.
How many characters fit on a line; roughly 45–75 is most readable.
A set of harmonious font sizes used consistently across a design.
Display type is large and characterful (headlines); body type is set for comfortable reading.
The limited, deliberate set of colors a design uses.
A single stronger color reserved for the most important actions, used sparingly.
A color's base tone, its intensity, and how light or dark it is.
A color mixed with white (tint) or black (shade).
A legibility measure between text and background; WCAG AA needs ≥4.5:1 for body text.
A palette built from one hue (plus neutrals), letting weight and space carry hierarchy (e.g. Linear).
Padding is space inside an element's edge; margin is space outside it.
A consistent set of spacing values (e.g. 4 / 8 / 16 / 24 / 32px) reused everywhere.
Consistent vertical spacing that makes a page feel even and calm.
How much content is packed into a given amount of space.
The large, attention-grabbing section at the top of a page.
The button or link prompting the main action, e.g. ‘Get started’.
A contained block grouping related content (image, title, text).
A focused window that appears over the page to capture attention or input.
A list of sections that expand and collapse to reveal content.
A rotating set of slides or items shown in one space.
A small label above a heading giving context — overused, so use sparingly.
Named horizontal rows (Newest / Popular / themed) that frame a big catalog as a browsable feed (e.g. 21st.dev).
A keyboard-driven search/action menu (often ⌘K), common in pro tools.
A row of small preview thumbnails under a gallery or lightbox that shows every item and lets you jump straight to any one — also called a thumbnail rail or scrubber.
UX is how it works and feels (the experience); UI is how it looks (the surface).
How content is organized, structured, and labeled.
A low-detail, gray-box blueprint of a layout.
A high-detail static visual of the finished design.
A clickable, interactive version used to test flows.
How finished and detailed a design artifact is.
The path a user takes to complete a task.
A visual cue that signals how something works (a button that looks pressable).
Revealing information gradually so users aren't overwhelmed.
The mental effort a design demands; less is usually better.
A documented set of reusable components, rules, and styles that keep a product consistent.
Named values (colors, spacing, fonts) stored once and reused everywhere.
Documentation of how a brand's visuals and voice should be used.
A collection of pre-built, reusable UI pieces.
The industry-standard collaborative interface-design tool.

glossaries.label: Web Tech Basics
glossaries.intro: The technical building blocks behind every website — enough to understand how your designs actually get built and to talk to developers (or to me).

terms.def: The markup language that structures a web page's content.

The building blocks of HTML, written with tags like

…

.
Extra info on a tag (e.g. href on a link, alt on an image).
Using meaningful tags (<header>, <nav>, <article>) so structure is clear to browsers and screen readers.
Ranked headings that outline a page; one h1 per page is best practice.
Generic containers: div for blocks, span for inline bits.
The <a> tag that links to other pages or sections.
Embeds a picture; alt text describes it for accessibility and SEO.
Elements that collect user data — text fields, checkboxes, buttons.
The language that styles HTML: colors, spacing, layout, and type.
The part of a CSS rule that targets which elements to style.
Hooks for styling: classes are reusable, IDs are unique to one element.
Every element is a box made of content, padding, border, and margin.
How an element lays out: block, inline, flex, grid, or none.
A state-based selector like :hover or :focus.
Reusable values like --color-brand defined once and used everywhere.
Smoothly animate changes; move, scale, or rotate elements.
What happens when content is bigger than its box — scroll, hidden, or visible.
A 1-dimensional layout system for arranging items in a row or a column.
A 2-dimensional layout system for rows and columns together.
relative / absolute / fixed / sticky control where an element sits.

terms.term: Units (px / rem / em / % / vw / vh / ch)
terms.def: Ways to size things; rem scales with the root font size, vw/vh with the viewport.
Sets a value that flexes between a minimum and maximum — great for fluid type.
A rule that applies styles only at certain screen sizes (breakpoints).
A screen width at which the layout changes.
The line in the HTML head that makes a page scale correctly on mobile.
Writing base styles for small screens, then adding for larger ones.
The site's address, e.g. yoursite.com.
The computer that stores and serves your site's files to visitors.
Frontend is what users see and interact with; backend is the server and data behind it.
The browser's live, tree-shaped model of the page that JavaScript can read and change.
The language that makes pages interactive.
Tools that help build complex interfaces from reusable components.
A reusable, self-contained piece of UI.
A way for software to request data or actions from another system.
Version control that tracks changes and lets people collaborate without overwriting each other.
Built-in browser panels for inspecting and tweaking a page live.
Vectors (SVG) scale infinitely; rasters (JPG/PNG) are fixed pixel grids.
Scalable vector graphic — ideal for logos and icons.
Image formats: PNG for crisp/transparent, JPEG for photos, WebP for smaller modern files.
The little icon shown in the browser tab.
A compressed font format made for fast loading on the web.
Shrinking files so pages load faster.
A built-in browser tool that scores page speed, accessibility, and SEO.
Making sites usable for everyone, including people using assistive technology.
Practices that help a page rank in search results.

glossaries.label: Digital Ads
glossaries.intro: How digital advertising works — formats, sizes, the anatomy of a strong ad, the platforms and auction behind it, and the metrics that judge it.

terms.def: A static or animated image ad placed in a slot on a webpage.
An ad styled to match the surrounding content so it feels in-context.
An ad that appears inline in a social feed (Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn).
A full-screen vertical (9:16) ad shown between stories or short videos.
A video that plays before (pre-roll), during (mid-roll), or after content.
A swipeable series of images or cards inside one ad unit.
A full-screen ad that covers the app or page at a natural break.
An interactive, animated ad (expandable or playable) built in HTML5.
A text ad shown above the organic results for a search query.
A product ad with image, price, and merchant shown in search or a marketplace.
Full-page portrait unit sized for tablets held upright (e.g. iPad).
Full-page landscape unit for tablets held sideways.
Full-screen interstitial for phones held sideways.
A large, premium banner near the top of a page.
Full-screen portrait interstitial for phones.
A large, high-impact vertical unit.
The first moment (or first 3 seconds of video) that stops the scroll.
The single biggest promise or message in the ad.
The main visual — the product, the result, or the feeling.
The button or line telling the viewer exactly what to do ('Shop now').
Clear, consistent brand presence so the ad is attributable at a glance.
The one reason this is worth a click.
Ratings, reviews, or recognizable logos that build trust.
The area kept clear of platform UI overlays (e.g. the Stories caption/CTA strip).
Search, Display, YouTube, and Shopping ads across Google's network.
Facebook and Instagram ads managed in Meta Ads Manager.
Automated, real-time buying of ad space via software.
The tool advertisers use to buy programmatic inventory.
A broker that aggregates and resells ad space across many sites.
A model where you pay only when someone clicks.
Advertisers bid; an auction decides which ad shows and what it costs.
How many times the ad was shown.
How many unique people saw it.
Clicks divided by impressions — how compelling the ad is.
Cost per 1,000 impressions.
Cost per click.
Cost per acquisition — a sale or signup.
Revenue earned for every dollar spent — the headline efficiency number.
The share of clicks that complete the goal.
The average number of times one person saw the ad.
Whether the ad was actually in view, not just served.
Choosing who sees the ad by demographics, interests, or behavior.
New people who resemble your existing customers.
Re-showing ads to people who already visited you.
A snippet on your site that tracks visits and conversions.
Tags added to a link that tell analytics where a click came from.
Deciding which ad or touchpoint gets credit for a conversion.
Awareness then Consideration then Conversion — ads are tailored to each stage.
Each impression is auctioned in milliseconds as the page loads.
The page after the click should deliver on the ad's promise.
Running variants to find the best-performing creative.
Performance drops as people see the same ad too often; refresh the creative.
